blocks.text: The **Internet of Things (IoT)** connects billions of everyday physical objects to the network so they can sense, act, and exchange data. This power comes with a hard problem: many of these devices are cheap, tiny, long-lived, and physically exposed, which makes them very difficult to secure. This lesson builds the topic from zero, following the professor's terminology and the ITU-T standards used in the slides.
1. What is the IoT?

The current Internet connects countless devices through unified communication frameworks to deliver services directly to users. Experts predict that within a few years **tens of billions** of devices will be connected.

The key idea: IoT bridges the **physical world** with computing power, digital media, data analytics, applications, and services. It benefits many sectors: healthcare, home automation, energy, agriculture, transportation, environment, inventory, security, and education.
A **global infrastructure** that connects physical and virtual *things* via interoperable communication technologies to enable advanced services.

- A **Thing** is any identifiable physical or virtual object linked to communication networks.
- An **IoT Device** is a thing that **must support communication**, and *may* also include sensing, actuation, data capture, storage, and processing.

Put simply, IoT combines four ingredients: **physical objects, controllers, sensors, actuators, and the Internet**. The controller (a microcontroller) provides the intelligence, the sensors/actuators interface with the physical world, and the Internet ties everything together so data and commands can flow.

blocks.title: Communication is the non-negotiable part
blocks.text: An IoT device **may** lack sensors or actuators, but it **cannot** lack communication. Without the ability to communicate, a device simply cannot join a network and is not an IoT device. The other parts (sensing, processing, storage) are optional.

**The new dimension of connection.** Traditional ICT gave us communication *any time* and *from any location*. IoT adds a third axis: communication among **any thing**. That "any thing" axis is what makes the attack surface explode.
2. Components of an IoT thing

Picture a single smart object. Its typical building blocks are:

- a **microcontroller** (a small computer) that provides the "intelligence"
- **sensors** (read physical parameters: temperature, motion) and/or **actuators** (change the physical world: open a valve, switch a relay)
- **network connectivity**, usually via a **transceiver** (radio that sends and receives)
- an **identity / ID** so it can be uniquely addressed

Most IoT devices have *some* computing power, even if very basic, plus one or more of the other parts.

question.prompt: According to ITU-T Y.2060, which capability must every IoT device support?
question.explanation: Y.2060 states an IoT device *must* support communication; sensing, actuation, storage, and processing are optional capabilities it *may* have. A device that cannot communicate cannot join the network, so communication is the defining requirement.

blocks.text: 3. The ITU-T IoT reference model

The ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union, Telecommunication Standardization Sector) organizes IoT into **four layers**, with **management** and **security** functions running *across all layers* (vertical, cutting through every level).

steps.label: Device layer
steps.text: The physical devices and how they connect. In OSI terms this maps mainly to the **physical and data-link layers**, handling fundamental connectivity.

steps.label: Network layer
steps.text: Transport and networking that moves data between devices and the core.

steps.label: Service support & application support layer
steps.text: Generic and specific support capabilities for applications.

steps.label: Application layer
steps.text: The actual IoT applications and services.

blocks.title: Two cross-cutting pillars
blocks.text: **Management capabilities** and **security capabilities** are not a single layer at the bottom; they span all four layers. The **security capability layer** provides *generic* security features that stay **independent from the applications**, so the same protections can serve many different services.
4. The security landscape (logical framework)

At the network's core sit the **application platforms, data repositories, and the network/security management systems**. They collect sensor data, dispatch commands to actuators, and oversee the devices.

At the **edge** sit the IoT devices themselves, ranging from simple resource-limited units to advanced capable ones. **Gateways** sit between them and often perform **protocol translation** and provide extra networking services.

blocks.title: Don't assume edge devices are protected
blocks.text: Systems near the core (and gateways) typically support strong security protocols like **TLS** and **IPsec**. But **resource-limited devices usually have minimal or NO built-in security**, and even sophisticated devices *may* include security features though this is **not guaranteed**. The gateway is therefore crucial: it secures the link between itself and the central platforms. Treating the whole IoT chain as uniformly secure is a classic exam mistake.

A simplified version of the **World Forum IoT Reference Model** describes the framework in layers you should be able to name:

- **Smart Objects / embedded systems** (the edge devices)
- **Edge & Fog Network** (local connectivity)
- **Core Network** (long-haul transport)
- **Data Center / Cloud** (apps, storage, management)

steps.label: Smart Objects / Embedded
steps.text: Sensors, actuators, embedded devices at the edge. Often operate **without physical security**. Need continuous availability, authenticity and integrity of sensor data, protection against unauthorized access and eavesdropping.

steps.label: Edge & Fog Networks
steps.text: Wired/wireless connectivity linking devices. Main challenge: a **diverse mix** of network technologies and protocols, so enforcing one consistent security framework is hard.

steps.label: Core Network
steps.text: Carries data between central hubs and devices. Faces classic core-network risks, but the **huge number of endpoints** amplifies the complexity.

steps.label: Data Center / Cloud
steps.text: Hosts applications, data, and management. IoT adds **no fundamentally new** threats here, except the challenge of managing an enormous number of individual endpoints.

blocks.text: 4b. The four security capabilities (cross-layer)
Within this four-layered framework the slides single out **four security features that operate across multiple layers**. Learn them as a named set of exactly four - this is a common exam target:

- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** - instead of assigning permissions to individual users, RBAC allocates access rights to **roles**; users are then linked to those roles, permanently or temporarily, based on their duties. Widely adopted in commercial cloud and enterprise environments and a proven way to regulate access to IoT devices and the data they generate.
- **Tamper Resistance and Detection** - crucial primarily at the **device and fog** layers (but also applies to the **core network**), because components at these levels may sit physically **outside the enterprise's secured perimeter**, making tamper detection vital.
- **Data Security and Privacy** - implemented throughout **every layer** of the architecture to safeguard sensitive information.
- **Internet Protocol Security** - ensures the **confidentiality and integrity** of data as it travels **between layers**, preventing interception and unauthorized monitoring.

blocks.title: Match each capability to where it lives
blocks.text: RBAC governs *who* can access things; **Tamper Resistance/Detection** is physical and concentrated at device/fog/core (outside the secure perimeter); **Data Security & Privacy** spans *all* layers; **Internet Protocol Security** protects data *moving between* layers. If a question asks which capability protects against physical tampering of an exposed sensor, the answer is tamper resistance and detection - not encryption-in-transit.
5. Security requirements (ITU-T Y.2066)
Recommendation **Y.2066** lists the essential security requirements for IoT. Learn these as a checklist:

- **Communication Security** - secure, trustworthy, privacy-respecting communication; prevent unauthorized access; guarantee integrity in transit.
- **Data Management Security** - protect data *storage and processing*; keep data accurate and private at rest.
- **Service Provision Security** - deliver services securely and reliably; prevent fraud and misuse; protect user privacy.
- **Integration of Security Policies and Techniques** - combine diverse policies/methods into consistent protection across heterogeneous devices.
- **Mutual Authentication and Authorization** - device/user **and** platform each verify the other's identity and permissions *before* access.
- **Security Audit** - every access (or attempt) is transparent, traceable, reproducible, and logged across transmission, storage, processing, and use.

blocks.title: Memory hook: "both sides verify"
blocks.text: **Mutual** authentication means it is two-way. The device proves itself to the platform *and* the platform proves itself to the device. A rogue server that only checks the device (but is never checked itself) breaks the requirement.
6. Why IoT is so hard to secure
The slides describe IoT as one of the **most intricate and least mature** sectors in network security. Beyond cost and sheer scale, several structural challenges make it worse:

- **Unpredictable behavior** - massive numbers and diverse tech produce hard-to-anticipate real-world interactions. Even a carefully engineered, well-managed system stays risky because its **interactions with other systems remain uncertain**.
- **Device homogeneity** - many devices share designs, identical connectivity methods, and hardware components, so **one vulnerability affects many** (monoculture risk).
- **Deployment challenges** - IoT aims to push advanced networks and analytics into previously unreachable areas, but that places devices in unusual/easily accessible spots that are hard to protect physically.
- **Extended life & end-of-support** - devices outlive vendor support and become **"orphaned"** with no patches; traditional systems, by contrast, usually keep getting updates even after users abandon them.
- **Lack of upgrade** - many cannot be updated easily, and even when updates exist they are inconvenient to apply, so users ignore them.
- **Limited transparency** - users cannot see or control internal processes, have no control over unwanted features or data collection, and vendor updates can even add undesired functionality.
- **Absence of alerts** - IoT tries to be seamless and unobtrusive, so users don't actively monitor devices and breaches go unnoticed for long periods.

blocks.title: The orphaned smart camera
blocks.text: A home buys a smart security camera. It works reliably for 8 years. After year 3 the manufacturer **stops releasing firmware** (end of support). A new vulnerability is discovered in year 4. Because the camera is **homogeneous** (millions of identical units), attackers automate the exploit; because there are **no alerts** and the user never checks, the camera is silently recruited into a botnet for years. This single scenario combines four of the listed challenges: long life, end-of-support, homogeneity, and absence of alerts.
7. The main threats

The slides group cybersecurity threats in IoT into **three critical areas** - **device failures, cyberattacks, and unauthorized data access (data breaches)**:

- **Device failures** - automation controls safety- and property-critical systems; a fault in an IoT heating system could freeze and burst pipes in an empty house, causing water damage. This risk forces organizations to add robust safeguards.
- **Data breaches** - data is IoT's greatest asset *and* its biggest vulnerability (targeted for marketing exploitation, identity theft, wrongful incrimination, stalking, or plain malice). The same measures that stop attacks also mitigate data theft.
- **Cyber attacks** - a compromised device (a smart oven, a tampered fire-sprinkler system) can open the whole network and connected systems. Defenses focus on **securing the weakest link**, continuous monitoring, and controlled access.

The slides also call out **simple yet powerful strategies** to counter these threats:

- **Built-in security** - devices designed with security built directly into their **hardware and firmware**.
- **Encryption** - essential at the **manufacturing stage** *and* within user environments to safeguard data.
- **Risk analysis** - both individuals and organizations should evaluate potential risks when **designing or selecting** IoT systems.
- **Access control** - enforce strict **privilege levels and authentication** wherever feasible to limit unauthorized use.

question.prompt: A vendor sells 5 million identical smart thermostats. One firmware bug lets an attacker take remote control. Which IoT security challenge does this BEST illustrate?
question.explanation: Device homogeneity means many devices share the same design and components, so a single vulnerability scales to all of them at once. Limited transparency is about users not seeing internal behavior; absence of alerts is about breaches going unnoticed; service provision security is a Y.2066 requirement, not a challenge category.

blocks.text: 8. Countermeasures - the toolbox

The slides list the **essential security technologies in use** for IoT. Know the full set, then we define the most important ones and contrast the ones students confuse.

- data **encryption** techniques
- **password-based** protection
- dedicated **Hardware Security Modules (HSMs)**
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**
- **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)**
- comprehensive **data wiping** methods
- **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** certificates
- **biometric** verification systems
- specialized **Hardware Cryptographic Processors**
- distributed ledger technology (**Blockchain**)

Cryptographic methods **designed for resource-limited devices** (RFID tags, sensors, contactless smart cards, medical equipment). They improve/adapt the traditional algorithms used in Internet security protocols like IPsec and TLS to fit small devices. They still give robust security suitable for many applications, but **do not always fully leverage the balance** between security strength and operational efficiency.

blocks.title: Passwords are a poor fit for IoT
blocks.text: Passwords fail for IoT for two reasons: (1) constrained devices struggle to **store/process** them, and (2) passwords need **human input**, which breaks automated **machine-to-machine** authentication. So password-based protection alone is increasingly unviable for automated IoT data exchanges - prefer certificates (PKI), keys in hardware, and MFA.

blocks.term: TEE vs. Secure Element vs. HSM vs. Crypto-processor
blocks.text:
- **TEE (Trusted Execution Environment)** - a **hardware security extension of the CPU** that creates an isolated, shielded area to run critical code and sensitive data, isolated at hardware level from other software (e.g. ARM, Intel, AMD, Qualcomm). It reuses the main processor and is suitable across the entire IoT system's layers.
- **Secure Element** - a separate **tamper-proof hardware module** with smart-card-grade security; acts as the device **root of trust**, stores secrets so they are accessible only to authorized users/applications, manages the device's entire lifecycle, and supports remote **over-the-air** updates of credentials, patches, and protection features.
- **HSM (Hardware Security Module)** - a dedicated physical device (plug-in card or external unit connecting to computers/servers) that **generates keys inside the device**, stores and administers them, handles crypto and sensitive data securely, and offloads asymmetric/symmetric crypto from application servers.
- **Secure crypto-processor** - a chip exclusively for crypto, in a tamper-resistant package, ensuring decrypted data/instructions **never leave** the protected boundary; it is the foundational element of a security subsystem, removing the need to physically protect the rest of the subsystem.

blocks.title: TEE vs Secure Element in one line
blocks.text: A **TEE** carves a protected zone *inside the main CPU* (software isolation enforced by hardware). A **Secure Element** is a *separate dedicated chip*. TEE = part of the processor; Secure Element = its own tamper-proof module acting as root of trust.

- **MFA / 2FA** - require **two distinct forms** of identification, unlike single-factor (SFA) which uses just one credential (usually a password); a long-trusted way to sharply raise the bar for attackers and protect critical systems and confidential information.
- **PKI certificates** - the roles, policies, and procedures to generate, manage, distribute, use, store, and **revoke digital certificates** and oversee public-key encryption; enables secure e-commerce, online banking, and confidential email, and is used where simple password authentication is inadequate and strong identity proof + data integrity are needed.
- **Biometrics** - match a person's unique physical/behavioral traits against a stored template. The reference template is first **enrolled** into a secure database or a protected portable device (e.g. a smart card); at authentication time the system compares the **live biometric input** against that stored template to confirm identity with high accuracy.

- **Tamper resistance & detection** - vital at device and fog layers (and core) because components sit **outside the secured perimeter**; the device must resist and detect physical tampering.
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)** - assign permissions to **roles**, not individual users; users are then mapped to roles (permanently or temporarily) by duty. Widely used in cloud/enterprise to govern IoT access.
- **Data erasure (wiping / clearing)** - a software- or hardware-based technique that overwrites existing data on storage (e.g. hard drives) with binary data (zeros and ones) so the original is unrecoverable, sanitizing the medium. Good techniques should offer a choice of **standards** and **confirm the overwrite covered the entire device**.
- **Blockchain for IoT** - a decentralized, append-only database/ledger: no single central computer controls it, every node holds a full copy, and records are only added, never removed. It is built from **transactions** (user-initiated activities) grouped into time-stamped **blocks** that are chronologically ordered and tamper-evident. It offers transparency, robustness against failures, auditability, and efficiency; integrating it with IoT improves data confidentiality/integrity and lets devices **detect and counter tampering**, building trust between parties.

question.prompt: An IoT chip designer wants secrets and crypto code to run in an isolated region of the device's MAIN application processor, protected by hardware from the normal OS, without adding a separate dedicated chip. Which technology fits best?
question.explanation: A TEE is a hardware security *extension of the CPU* that isolates critical code and data inside the main processor - exactly the requirement. A Secure Element and an HSM are *separate* dedicated hardware modules, not part of the main CPU. Data erasure is about sanitizing storage, not isolated execution.

question.prompt: Within the four-layered IoT framework, which cross-layer security capability is described as protecting confidentiality and integrity of data as it travels BETWEEN layers?
question.explanation: Internet Protocol Security ensures confidentiality and integrity of data moving between layers, preventing interception and unauthorized monitoring. RBAC governs who can access resources; Tamper Resistance and Detection is physical protection at device/fog/core layers; Data Security and Privacy spans every layer to safeguard sensitive information but is not specifically the in-transit, between-layers capability.

blocks.title: The big picture
blocks.text: IoT security is hard because devices are **constrained, homogeneous, long-lived, physically exposed, and rarely updated**. The standards give structure: **Y.2060** defines what IoT is, the **ITU-T 4-layer model** organizes the architecture with security as a cross-cutting pillar (RBAC, tamper resistance/detection, data security & privacy, Internet Protocol Security), and **Y.2066** lists the security requirements. The countermeasures - lightweight crypto, TEE/Secure Element/HSM/crypto-processor, mutual authentication via PKI, MFA, biometrics, RBAC, tamper resistance, data erasure, lifecycle management, and blockchain - are the concrete tools that satisfy those requirements on weak devices.

objectives: Define IoT, a Thing, and an IoT device using ITU-T Y.2060, and state the one mandatory capability
Describe the ITU-T four-layer IoT reference model and the World Forum framework layers, including security as a cross-cutting pillar
Name the four cross-layer security capabilities (RBAC, tamper resistance & detection, data security & privacy, Internet Protocol Security) and where each applies
List the ITU-T Y.2066 security requirements, including mutual authentication and security audit
Explain why constrained IoT devices are hard to secure (homogeneity, end-of-support, lack of upgrades, no alerts, physical exposure, limited transparency, unpredictable behavior)
Identify the main IoT threat areas (device failures, data breaches, cyber attacks) and the simple strategies that counter them (built-in security, encryption, risk analysis, access control)
Select appropriate countermeasures and distinguish TEE, Secure Element, HSM, crypto-processor, PKI, MFA, biometrics, RBAC, tamper resistance, data erasure, and blockchain
summary: A from-zero lesson on IoT security following the ITU-T standards from the slides: it defines IoT devices and their constraints, walks the four-layer reference model and security landscape, names the four cross-layer security capabilities, lists the Y.2066 security requirements, surveys the structural challenges, threats, and counter-strategies, and contrasts the full countermeasure toolbox (lightweight crypto, TEE vs Secure Element vs HSM vs crypto-processor, PKI, MFA, biometrics, RBAC, tamper resistance, data erasure, blockchain).
title: Module G: IoT security
